topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom air felt thick, suffocating, like wading through syrup. Every laugh from the reception seemed amplified, sharp edges cutting through the din. I kept scanning the periphery, my eyes darting from the glittering chandeliers to the shadows pooling in the corners of the room. Then I saw him. Standing near the champagne fountain, bathed in a stray shaft of light, was Alex. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake that felt too loud in my own ears. My hands tightened around my glass, knuckles white. It wasn't just seeing him; it was a sudden, visceral jolt, a feeling that the carefully constructed reality of the evening was about to fracture. I immediately started backing toward the exit, a jerky, almost panicked movement, pretending to adjust my jacket while my entire focus was locked on his silhouette.

[story 2]

"Just a moment," I mumbled to myself, forcing a casual smile as I navigated the crowded dance floor. My senses were on high alert, every unfamiliar face registering as a potential threat. I watched the flow of people, cataloging exits and potential hiding spots. And then, there he was. Across the room, laughing with someone I vaguely recognized, was Sarah. My stomach clenched instantly, a cold, tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I slowed my pace, deliberately bumping into a waiter, just to create a momentary distraction, my gaze flicking back to her. The smile felt brittle, stretched thin over a core of intense unease. I needed to leave, needed distance, needed to erase this unwelcome intrusion from my immediate vicinity.

[story 3]

The music was a relentless, cheerful assault, and I found myself clinging to the nearest pillar, trying to anchor myself against the swirling chaos. My vision tunneled slightly; the faces blurred into indistinct shapes, all of them suddenly carrying an unnerving intensity. Then, across the expanse of polished floor, I spotted him. Liam. He was talking animatedly, and for a split second, the noise of the party seemed to drop away, leaving only a high-pitched ringing in my ears. A strange, icy sensation washed over me, making my skin prickle. I didn't move, didn't breathe deeply, just watched, cataloging every micro-expression, every shift in his posture, convinced that the air itself had turned hostile. I felt an overwhelming urge to simply vanish, to dissolve into the wallpaper.
